title: Rust on Cotton
stainChemistry: Rust on cotton is iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) physically embedded in the fiber and mechanically bound to cellulose. Unlike organic stains, rust is a MINERAL — soap, enzymes, oxidizers (peroxide, OxiClean, chlorine bleach) do almost nothing against it. Worse: chlorine bleach on rust makes the stain PERMANENT by oxidizing Fe²⁺ to a harder-to-reduce form. The only chemistry that moves rust is acid — dilute oxalic acid (pro / hardware-store rust remover) or citric acid (home) chelates and reduces iron to a soluble form that can be rinsed out. Cotton tolerates these acids at dilute concentrations.
whyThisWorks: Rust responds to acid reduction, not detergent or oxidation. Dilute oxalic acid (Whink / Rust Stain Magic / Bar Keepers Friend) or lemon juice + salt (home equivalent) chelates the iron and reverses the oxidation. With the iron in soluble form, NSD rinses it out. The hard no-chlorine-bleach rule is absolute — bleach on rust = permanent orange-brown mark.
defaultAssumption: Rust on cotton requires acid chemistry, not the standard stain toolkit. Assume the user's first instinct will be bleach or OxiClean — warn them off that immediately. Fresh rust = likely DIY with lemon juice + salt. Set-in rust may need commercial rust remover.

spottingProtocol.technique: Dilute oxalic acid or lemon juice
spottingProtocol.equipment: Commercial rust remover (Whink, Bar Keepers Friend) OR lemon juice + salt
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply dilute oxalic-acid rust remover to the spot per the product label. OR make a home version: sprinkle salt on the rust, squeeze lemon juice over until wet, let sit 10 minutes. The acid reduces iron oxide to a soluble iron salt. Rinse cool.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. Flushes the now-soluble iron out of the fiber.
Set-in rust often needs 2–3 acid + NSD cycles. Each pass reduces another layer.
Rinse until water runs clear. Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.

steps: 1. Dilute oxalic-acid rust remover — dwell 5–15 min. Rinse cool.
3. Repeat acid + NSD cycle for set-in rust (2–3 passes).
products: Commercial oxalic-acid rust remover (Whink, Rust Stain Magic, Bar Keepers Friend)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: NEVER use chlorine bleach on rust — makes the stain permanent
Oxalic acid is a skin/eye irritant — wear gloves + eye protection for pro-strength
Detergents, enzymes, peroxide are all ineffective on rust — acid is required
Ventilate when using commercial rust remover

homeSolutions.agent: DO NOT Use Bleach or OxiClean
homeSolutions.instruction: Before you do anything: rust is a MINERAL stain, not an organic one. Chlorine bleach and OxiClean — which are your default for most cotton stains — do NOTHING on rust, and bleach specifically can make rust stains PERMANENT by oxidizing the iron to a form that can't be reduced. Soap, enzyme, and peroxide are also useless. Rust needs acid chemistry.

homeSolutions.agent: Lemon Juice + Salt — the Home Rust Remover
homeSolutions.instruction: Sprinkle table salt directly on the rust stain. Squeeze fresh lemon juice over the salt until the spot is wet. Let it sit 10 minutes in direct sunlight if possible — sun accelerates the reaction. The citric acid chelates (binds to) the iron and reduces it to a soluble form that rinses out.

Rinse the salt + lemon juice out with cool water. The rust should be lighter. Light or fresh rust often clears in one pass; set-in rust may need 2–3 cycles. Re-apply salt + lemon juice if color remains.

homeSolutions.agent: Commercial Rust Remover for Stubborn Stains
homeSolutions.instruction: For rust that's been on cotton a long time or isn't responding to lemon juice: use a commercial rust remover — Whink Rust Stain Remover, Iron OUT, or Bar Keepers Friend (apply as a paste). Follow the product label exactly. These use oxalic acid, which is more effective than citric acid but also more caustic.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Commercial rust removers are skin and eye irritants. Wear gloves. Work in a ventilated space. Never mix with bleach or ammonia. Keep away from children.

homeSolutions.instruction: Once the rust is gone: work a drop of Dawn into the spot to remove any remaining acid, rinse, then launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Any remaining rust that touches hot water might come back out, but hot water alone won't clear it — you need the acid step first.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Persistent or Ironed-In Rust
homeSolutions.instruction: If the rust has been through multiple hot-dryer cycles before treatment, it may have been fixed by bleach already applied, or two home rounds haven't cleared it, take to a dry cleaner. They have stronger reducing agents. Tell them: "Rust on cotton, age [fresh / old / long-standing], home-treated with [lemon juice + salt / Whink / Bar Keepers Friend]. Bleach [used / never used]."

steps: 1. NO BLEACH, NO OXICLEAN, NO PEROXIDE — none of these work on rust; bleach makes it permanent.
2. Salt + lemon juice on the stain, 10 min in sun if possible.
3. Rinse cool. Repeat for set-in rust.
4. Commercial rust remover (Whink / Iron OUT / Bar Keepers Friend) for stubborn stains — follow label, wear gloves.
5. Dawn + hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
6. Escalate if bleach was applied previously or home round fails.
products: Commercial oxalic-acid rust remover (Whink Rust Stain Remover, Iron OUT, Bar Keepers Friend)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if chlorine bleach has already been applied to the rust (may be permanent), if rust has been through multiple hot dryer cycles, or if two home rounds haven't cleared it.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach on rust — can make it permanent
NEVER OxiClean, peroxide, or enzyme — all ineffective
Oxalic-acid rust removers are caustic — gloves, ventilation, no contact with bleach or ammonia
Set-in rust may need multiple cycles

neverDo: NEVER chlorine bleach on rust — permanent oxidation
NEVER mix rust remover with bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk
Never skip gloves when using commercial rust remover
Never use rust remover on colored fabric without an inside-seam test — can strip dye
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates dilute oxalic acid and citric acid at room temperature
Dyed cotton: commercial rust remover can strip some dyes — test inside seam first
Heat-durable fiber; the rust chemistry, not the fiber, is the variable

materialWarnings: RUST NEEDS ACID — soap/enzyme/peroxide all useless
Commercial rust remover is caustic — gloves + ventilation

escalation.when: Chlorine bleach was applied to the rust previously (may be permanent), rust has been through multiple hot dryer cycles, or two home rounds haven't cleared it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Rust on cotton, age [fresh / old / long-standing], home-treated with [lemon juice + salt / Whink / Bar Keepers Friend]. Bleach [used / never used on this stain]."

contextQuestions: Has chlorine bleach been applied to this stain? (important — may be permanent)
What caused the rust — iron pipes, tools, metal zipper, iron in water?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The "never use bleach" rule is the home-treatment unlock. Most people default to bleach for any stubborn cotton stain and with rust it's actively harmful. Acid chemistry is the specific lane.
